Everyone should stake relationships on whether existence is good :^) and also on the semantic representation of this idea. There is a viewpoint among some Christians that you should be willing to reach out and even share meals with those who do not yet confess the name of Christ, but should not even speak to professing Christians who identify in death. A lot of this is about clarity and proper signs. Improper signs make for the inability of a community to orient toward existence.

I personally draw the line a little looser and would only not speak to people who use the name of Christ but are explicitly heretics, like Mormons and Jehovah's Witnesses. Narcissism of small differences is actually good when the boundary between truth and falsehood is hard to discern without enforcement of boundaries.

It is kinda like how I have no strong need to really enforce a boundary between myself and non-ethnic-Chinese-Canadians because literally no one would mistake what I am all about with what they are all about. But I do enforce a strong boundary between myself and both Chinese-Canadians (when speaking to ethnic Chinese) and all ethnic Chinese in general (when speaking to non-Chinese people). In my view, it is actually rational to spend more effort on the particulars and schism points.

Tribalism is good if your tribe is ultimately winning, and is bad if your tribe is ultimately losing. This will mean that any overly reified tribe is going to be bad at some point since it will dig in its heels in the face of some distance from the way and the truth and the life, but if your tribe is existence itself and all those who graft into existence, you have no reason to *not* be extremely tribal.

My own tribalism is more about the signs than the hidden significance as interpreted because in the end I cannot read anyone's mind and I am only operating on top of signs. So all at once I am happy to speak against signs I consider to be wrong, but also hold the exact human membership boundary quite loosely unless it concerns something for corporate organisms like the visible church.
Mathematics is to axioms what theology is to creeds.

Now is a good time to say that most mistakes with institution formation have to do not with having too wide of a moat, but rather not wide enough of one. The other side of the coin is spending a lot of quality time outside of the moat engaging with the world.

<@1221182134950301706> You might want to consider what short list of visible signs you want people to affirm in ORI, haha. 

Of course to some degree this might undermine a desire to be post-paradigmatic, but to be honest I do not think that this can be achieved unless there were way better translation technology to the point where all speech was utterly seamless.
what does it mean to enforce a strong boundary between you and Chinese Canadians or ethnic Chinese

I personally propose that at the very least, ORI membership should require thinking that existence (whatever it is) is good, that existence (whatever it is) can be modelled as having a will, and everyone should want to align their will to that of reality. :^)

I predict that instituting this policy of affirmation (essentially creedal but with more secular language) would immediately decrease the amount of degeneracy that finds its way into ORI. Mostly thinking of the deal with Hephaistos, but Shane leaving also triggered a thought on what boundaries are needed to keep the peace. <@335107602243518466>

I am trying to be generous and consider what interesting theologies can be created downstream of the supposition that existence is not necessarily good (many "Christian" theologies try to do this) but I will be honest, it is quite difficult to imagine someone not willing to say that existence is good and then not having some weird hangup.
imo a rule should be you can't call people idiots
Counterintuitively we tend to make attacks on competence more acceptable than attacks on alignment (good vs. evil) when I think it should be the other way around.
It is better to demonize than to minimize, in many regards! At least, I think this is true in an open discourse platform.

(PROVISIONAL) Open Research Institute Membership Affirmations (Sunrise Edition)

1. Existence is good.
2. One ought to pursue what is good.
3. Open Research Institute members are not idiots.
There could be tension between 1, 2, and 3 in that ORI might actually have an idiot in which case ORI is misaligned in not considering them idiots, but...
Probably best to not make an explicit exhortation too high up on the ladder compared to baseline worldview points that are basic to visible alignment

I think

3. crashing out is fine and a normal part of being human, sleep on it before taking drastic action
Yeah I think these are fine as guidelines; maybe that should be separate
One is a core constitution of affirmation and the other is a set of visible norms that could be broken but only by direct appeal to the affirmations
can have a private channel for virtual fist fights to hash things out

The problem with making "Calling people idiots is bad" and "You ought to sleep on decisions before taking drastic action if evaluated to be 'crashing out'" into actual symbolic slignment points is that you end up creating first-century Judean culture rather than the Law of Moses. If that makes sense.
I think "keep things light and civil" is a very common rule in discord servers

This is where most Discord servers are actually misaligned, though; making "keep things light and civil" a *core value or rule* rather than a guideline mega reifies the interpretation of what lightness and civility are. I think creedal approaches are more fair LOL

Some rules can help you to keep the core significance behind more core affirmations, but making the rules themselves into core elements of identity is going to get you into a situation where you think Jesus is a sinner because he healed someone on the Sabbath.
Yeah, I think as guidelines these are good to lay out

Just that I think the binding rule should actually be on explicit symbolic affirmations. Lifestyle and behavior are also symbols, of course, so they should affect how earnestly we think someone affirms certain statements.

This is actually how discipline in the church basically goes. On the higher level, no specific visible behavior is actually hard unconditionally banned by Christian faith (in fact, all is available to the believer!), but one can be taken up the chain of questioning to the point where one is no longer believed to actually adhere to the faith or at least that communion.

And I think my focus right now is on the "bigger ORI". The ORI that is being self assembled. The ORI where you learn in public and you reach the frontier and then you get to contribute & test your ideas.

The group we have here is interested in developing & using the protocol. Everyone who's here is here because they're useful to me (they're either contributing to the protocol, or they are embodying it, and we can see if it works for them)

My follow up to this is that sometimes there are the tools and potential good outcome, but it looks like no one is interested or it’s buried under hundreds of thousands of academia papers. Remember skin-brain research that I was really interested in? Couldn’t find lab space and after assembling my lab the weight of it was too big for the capacity of the equipment with the angel grant I was given. Later, I realized I was obsessed with morphologies, embryology, genetic engineering and editing which is what I’m trying to pursue now. It’s aligning. I also asked to be an observer at my universities embryology lab.

So it’s working out better than I expected. I look at snails, slugs, planaria, anything soft and small and translucent to observe at my home lab. I got a tip from someone really interested to see if I can make some work on insect chimeras as well.
Prove on the littlest thing and keep moving up.

Always have wondered where true and useless goes in terms of the ABU review. It's like the information is not useful at this point of time but I can't determine if it'll be useful in the future.

Based on the example given, it most likely goes into B. Or even a U.

For the subjective epistemology part, I agree with 2 and 3.
1. might run into the correlation is not causation.
Propose something along the lines of, the more we can build and abstract away from it without loss of generality and the more cases that it can be applied to the more likely it is to be true.

So the ultimate truth is a statement that can be applied universally and is not domain specific. In a way 3 is a corollary of 1 if 1 was defined slightly better?
if you don't see a way to put it in your world model, it's a U

> So the ultimate truth is a statement that can be applied universally and is not domain specific. In a way 3 is a corollary of 1 if 1 was defined slightly better?

I am open to rewording this better - I kind of threw this in quickly, I spent more time on the ABU page
but I think the important point is that you can't be in ORI if you have not developed your own way of discerning truth yourself
doesn't matter how you do it, you just need to have _a process_
Oh it fits in the world model, but it's kinda just there and I haven't found a use for it yet. It's just sitting there as a passive not yet validated state. so it's like a b/u
yeah, there's another interesting concept here which is, WHEN does something go from A to B?
I used to think it was "instant". Like it's A upon contact, and then the next time you query/bring it up, it's B
Hmm yeah that's fair, like a checksum.

but I'm now thinking there might be something where something stays as "A" until it has something built upon it. It's like imagine your knowledge is a grid, everything that is surrounded by something else is B, and anything on the edge, in any direction, is A
like your knowledge is B on the inside, wrapped in a shell of A's
Based on how I view information processing, it doesn't become a B unless you can trigger an A in someone else that has your B floor

that's how I would see it in terms of peer review. I think that's a great, precise standard

(I just started thinking last night, "if you are not doing peer review, are you doing science?" and I started thinking about feedback from reality (correct prediction) vs feedback from another mind)

Until you can control the information, it's hasn't completely taken root yet. You just have the tool, don't know how to use it yet or started to master it.

One of my fundamental beliefs is that you don't own what you can't give others.
So you don't own the revelation, understanding or blessing if you can't give it away to others

I am speaking primarily about visible association, so it is not about better or worse. The true "worse" is all worst in the context of community and communion, in the sense of not being associated with presumption of salvation. 

But also, regarding the visible signs vs. the significance...

Non-Trinitarianism *is* atheism, in some respects. But we can also say that someone not understanding the signs is different than rejecting the signs. I think that I have less assurance that someone might understand the significance behind the signs if they merely do not understand the Trinity than if they actively reject it. I do actually think Mormons have some degree of insight about God, but they in rejecting the formulation of the three persons and the one essence makes them worse to *associate with* if the church cares about testimony and messaging.

It is not enough to point to the "what" of Christianity as Muslims do, or merely use the names of the "whos" of Christianity as Mormons do. One must be worshipping the same essence and the same persons to be presumed to be within the communion of saints expressed within the Apostles' Creed.

Right, in euler's identity is compression algorithm for 3D geometry

e = continuous growth/decay (the differential operator, the change)
i = orthogonal rotation (the 90-degree turn, the imaginary axis)
π = circularity/periodic return (the fundamental cycle)

These together = ANY rotation in 3D space = completes my Ruliad paper really

They compress down to -1, which when you add 1 gives you 0.

So the -1 here is actually the reference frame, the observer's position <@1250084478152478773> 

So Euler's identity is saying:
"A complete rotation (π) in the orthogonal dimension (i) with continuous transformation (e) brings you exactly opposite (-1) from your starting reference (1), which nets to the void (0)"

**1. Romans 1:18–23**
The definitive text. Humanity “suppresses the truth” though “what can be known about God is plain,” for “God has shown it.” The refusal is not ignorance but rebellion. The visible world is evidence; unbelief is the rejection of reality’s testimony.

**2. Psalm 14:1 / 53:1**
“The fool says in his heart, ‘There is no God.’”
Folly is not low IQ but moral insanity—the denial of the most basic ontological fact.

**3. John 1:9–11**
“The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not comprehended it… He came to His own, and His own did not receive Him.”
The Light is ontological: Christ is the ground of all being. Yet darkness clings to its own blindness.

**4. John 3:19–20**
“People loved the darkness rather than the light because their deeds were evil.”
Unbelief is not epistemic failure but moral evasion. They prefer non-reality because the Light exposes.

**5. 2 Thessalonians 2:10–12**
“They refused to love the truth… Therefore God sends them a strong delusion, so that they may believe what is false.”
The refusal to acknowledge truth yields submersion into unreality—delusion as judgment.

**6. Ephesians 4:17–19**
The Gentiles “are darkened in their understanding… alienated from the life of God… due to the hardness of their heart.”
Cognitive decay follows spiritual rupture; hardness produces blindness.

**7. 2 Peter 3:3–5**
Scoffers “deliberately overlook” the plain facts of creation and judgment.
Their ignorance is active, not passive—an intentional bracketing of reality.

**8. Isaiah 6:9–10**
“Keep on hearing, but do not understand; keep on seeing, but do not perceive.”
A judicial hardening that makes the obvious opaque.

**9. Jeremiah 5:21**
Hear this, “O foolish and senseless people, who have eyes but see not, who have ears but hear not.”
A refusal to perceive what perception was made for.

**10. Proverbs 12:15**
“The way of a fool is right in his own eyes.”
The delusion is internally self-validating—closed to the external order that would correct it.

there's a few options here. 

- can copy this 1:1 as a basic custom HTML website, can do free hosting on github (or can buy a domain). It'd be some upfront work but could make it as something other people can re-use in the future
- use mdBook (write your chapters as markdown, it gets published like this: https://openresearchinstitute.org/onboarding/ ) - can include a PDF that's generated / link to download it on the main page
- use Quartz (same process as mdbook, you write in markdown, and it generates a little book/website. This set of templates is designed to work with Obsidian as an editor). Example 1:  https://toolbox.socratica.info/, Example 2: https://turntrout.com/welcome
- could technically just make a Substack publication dedicated to this, but you'd have to manually be generating the pdf. I think the markdown routes give you a way to automate generating the right files and having it ready to download/sell

Yeah, this personalization of monotheistic God is were I'm struggling, but I think I'm starting to see it. In particular, I see personality more as a delta and once you have God as a giant lump of everything you seemingly lose that delta. I suppose that's where the devil comes in to serve as a reference.

From what I can tell, the personality of God is love, but it's not quite like the human love. It's more about a pervading optimal connectedness between everything at various scopes. A propensity for favoring existence as to nudge things to coalesce and interact.

In a funny way, our kinimatics equations and other physics math describes God's personality in quite a detailed and fairly lossless way, granted it's still a collapse, but it seems to have constrained some dimension of God.

At the same time, I think this conceptualization is too hard for most people to understand so the personification is used to project the self, which is indeed part of god, but problems arise when this projection is assuming to be whole. But, if you can manage to see God's personality without projection, you can probably get quite a good handle on it. I think Dao comes close in trying to remove the human projections and give attention to more primordial principles.

So, I think there's quite a strange and more connected or less failure prone path to God personification through Dao that other more human projection based approachs often fall short.

I think that the issue of conflating the self with the other personality is something that we cannot *anywhere*; but the fact of projection and of inability to develop an exhaustive or comprehensive understanding (we cannot even fully understand one merely human person!) does not mean that one would be better off not personifying. In fact, we could say that it is precisely the type of person who tends to read himself into personalities who is the exact type who might be better off dogmatically believing that other people are like him (typical mind fallacy), because the other approach is dehumanization and lack of relationship altogether. It is better for someone to think of God as a man in the sky than to not relate to God.

Putting it another way, even though human love is not the ideal love and human wrath is not the ideal wrath, love and wrath are perfectly coincident in God; in fact, they are the same thing (divine simplicity). Either way, it is a desire (fully fulfilled in God) for existence, and by corollary a full rejection of non-existence. When multiple minds all seek to participate in existence, they naturally become extensions of each other, just like how my body and my organs (and even my family and friends) *are* me in the sense that they serve my will and expand my scope of influence. So I am both in Christ via his body, and extended through that same body. When I serve the ideal that other Christians are serving (God, who brings us to himself), other Christians are also serving my will. When Christians serve the save ideals that I do, they are doing *my* will. And this is completely without coercion, since we indeed want to serve that same will.

Just like how any mere human person is not who we say they are but in fact what they are (even though we only interface through our projection of who they are, filtered through our biased sampling), Christ (who is the way, the truth, and the life) is exactly what he is no matter what we think he is. Everyone is in a different place in their relationship to Christ.

And it must be said that though the normative way of knowing Christ is to encounter Jesus of Nazareth through the scriptures (as compiled and preserved in the Bible we received through history), one could just as easily find Christ (without the words for it) first through indwelling of the Holy Spirit and then see that what Jesus coherently claims to be in the scriptures is indeed the Christ.

basically, as you damage outputs, the player gets better at defending your sabotage so your EV of attacking outputs goes down over time. But if you can destabilize their ability to adapt their outputs to reality, you have a compounding advantage over time.
so because of this, the only natural outcome is that they converge on damaging the adaptability instead of the outputs.

Human mathematics was invented for us to discover mathematics 😉

Before I became a Christian, one of my catchphrases was that mathematics in the sense of adherence to unfalsifiable axioms and postulates (even if useful) is a religion. This was actually one of the lines of reasoning that got me to seriously consider wisdom traditions as legitimate.

Oh, it is also worth considering how some things which might not be "true" in a systematic sense help us to reconcile to existence. It is fun to think about the usage of "true" in love songs; what does it mean for your lover to be true to you?

My pastor says many things in Bible studies that are factually or scientifically  (for example the "[soul is 21 grams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/21_grams_experiment)" trope), but I don't correct him because I see that it functions correctly in getting older and less rigor-oriented people in the congregation to take the afterlife more seriously.

One of the internal crises of Christianity is that many of the modernist methods like historical criticism are utterly rejected by most faithful Christians and operated mostly by people who read the results as explaining away the faith and tradition. So I think outside of maybe the Catholic Church and maybe some extremely autistic scholastic circles in other traditions, it is quite hard to see that much engagement on epistemology and hermeneutics. Though, I think a lot of this is just because it is embedded a certain way and they cannot unpack it into the popular ontology. Similar to how one not immersed in Christianity cannot see how "fully man, fully God" relates to epistemology.

Most folk Christians intuit that how you interpret something is indicative of the spirit that is indwelling you, but sound like rambling schizophrenics when they say that someone is beset by demons. 😂  I think they actually have directionally right idea when it comes to the question of hermeneutics, but either do not have sophisticated language for it or are too used to this kind of talk being a slow burn ending with the other person saying, "So anyway, I want a lesbian pastor."

I am more sympathetic than most contemporary theological conservatives to some approaches used by theological liberals to justify things like women's ordination (I think someone being unfit for a while...and yes, women are categorically unfit to be presbyters...does not mean that they cannot validly hold the office). But man do I feel the same urge to roll my eyes and retreat into the rolling plains when I hear the absolute worst ideologically captured people use extremely dumb readings to say that the Bible does not condemn same-sex relationships in any way (or at least is meant to not be read that way). The fact that I might wrestle with their argument with a discussion on hermeneutics and some historical lenses and maybe some word studies is honestly a rounding error to the core issue that they are just wrong on this. And so honestly, I think some of the Christians with the best epistemic practices are just dumb as bricks.

I think there is actually a lot of cultural wisdom in the whole joke of “That’s wild man. So anyway, did you catch the game last night?” Because often, people who want to seem clever are less interested in trying to learn arguments very deeply and more concerned about how to engage in cleverisms which only superficially tackle issues known within the discourse to push some new line of thinking. So honestly, deflection and shutdown is sometimes just better than engagement, just to keep their roots shallow as they should be. I just have a chronic desire to type at people, so it results in me getting in way more discussions on these issues than I probably should.

I am doing some self-reflection, and it honestly feels like the last A I experienced was just sometime after my conversion when I “got it” regarding the sacraments and then this trickled down to all other areas of my philosophy and thought. I usually do not compartmentalize intellectual interests that much, so I expect that most of the things I see which I would truly consider to be As would be theological in nature.
I recommend this video to anyone who is interested in a crash course in what I am talking about re: sacraments.

I think core theological and foundational value improvements end up resulting in way larger upgrades in insight than pretty much any amount of tinkering downstream of it. There is so much leverage when changing things from the root! Of course, in practice, these insights often have to work their way to the root, because it is risky to abandon oneself (and this really is what Christ asks of you) and start over.

What if we abandon ourselves for the wrong thing? I think that the key to Christ is that must abandon ourselves to whoever *is* the right thing, because only the right thing is God. The one true God, the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, brings us into communion despite us walking blind without the gift of revelation.

But I realized that precisely because this stuff is obvious within my worldview (and within that of many Christian worldviews), we do not appreciate how misunderstanding of Christianity is actually quite rooted in a need for clear catechesis and not just encouragement in living a Christian life.

It really does come down to, every single A I can remember from my conversion to now is pretty much about theology. Nothing else is really that surprising, since simply operating a system from its premises using classical logic is an easy game even if it is hard to completely exhaust it. But meanwhile, developments in theology fundamentally orient me differently in what I do rely on, and so instantly ties together all lower systems (regardless of my level of mastery of them) and lets me settle into being a more integrated person within a continuously-sanctified body of Christ.

I wonder if this train of thought counts as a kind of self-induced A since I do actually feel the familiar headiness that comes from leaning into theological topics. But really, it was the ABU framework seemingly catching on that got me to think about it more seriously. So really, I think that ABU might count as an A for me, since it does fill something of a void in at least my own knowledge of how Christianity deals with knowledge gaps in those hearing Christian messages.
Yeah, let’s say that. The first confirmed A for me after hearing about ABU is probably ABU, after reflecting on when I last had an A experience.

This also is largely because I am in a particularly open state of mind right now, maybe. Just got over a flu so my brain opened up (I played piano terribly two days ago, now I am slick as ever), and I have been thinking in the back of my mind today about how to do better at getting the attention of larger audiences (inspired by [Defender’s relaying of Katt’s recent success](https://discord.com/channels/1344300408893345863/1344689281293221950/1446931471867842812)). Then I suddenly saw the [Bryan Johnson tweet about existence being the world’s reserve currency](https://x.com/bryan_johnson/status/1997450099069456490?s=20) and thought about what breadcrumbs I could leave around to get more attention. And the attention mindset is actually quite foreign to me! I am attention seeker usually on my *own* terms, not on someone else’s terms! I *can* model someone and whether they will find what I say interesting, but I am always mentally negotiating about how fun it would be to talk about what I want to think about! And right then, the algorithm showed me [Defender’s tweet seeing if people understand the ABU framework as applied to art](https://x.com/DefenderOfBasic/status/1997487684462518560?s=20), and after writing some comments on iconoclasm (still in my usual autistic fun mode) saw that someone bothered to create [an AI graphic of the ABU model](https://x.com/SeonGunness/status/1997490204559732757?s=20). This then got me to seriously think about how ABU applies to me.

Usually I feel in discussions others learn more about what I think than I learn about what they think because on top of liking to researching by myself on relevant topics as I chat with people I ask fairly pointed questions and also have an active evangelistic focus when speaking to non-Christians (can’t help it, and it feels inauthentic to shy away from it!), so this is is actually a pretty good experience to have learned something from ORI and its network just by virtue of what amounts to algorithmic fluke. God is good!

I think there is a lot of literal virtue (not just status "virtue") in maintaining ethereal networks which in theory could be completely vandalized, but which could also be easily backed up (or compressed), trivial to reverse-engineer if the right goals are established, and actively adaptive for people to interface with. This is a great mechanism for getting people to identify in the true, the good, the beautiful. 

Though, I will note, these conceptions do come close to making ORI into, in practice, a religion. Not that this has not been brought up before in the long discussions before and after the founding of ORI. "Open source religion" was an idea already. I do think that this comes to mind again because for the same reason that I am taking ABU more seriously due to seeing it catch on, I do have concerns on more central theological issues and disagreements that exist between the more prominent ORI members (and esp. between any areas where Defender might disagreement with the idea that he is better off submitting to Christ) since if people in practice treat ORI like a religion in the same way that communism or effective altruism or veganism are treated like religions, it can get dicey in my eyes if "Christian ORI" suffers. Of course, "Christian" anything will always be on its own doing its own thing.

I think a big potential difference between a "Christian ORI" and other modes of ORI is that Christianity is heavily schismatic in its outlook on humanity, which runs counter to the universalizing instincts of globalism, open culture, and even many modern religions like the Baha'i Faith. Christians can be very nice, but really at the end of the day Jesus brought not peace but a sword and has set those who love live against those who love death. So I think overall I adopt much more the attitude of cutting down that which visibly aligns to death down to size, while I think that Defender has the more generous attitude of trying to coax out the potential of the system and see if it can be realigned. 

Of course Christianity has also used this kind of cooperation (both "malicious" relative to the opposing intent and "benevolent" in the sense of helping the wellbeing of those following the discourse) to allow systems to grow. Not only is it directly in the scriptures with Jesus saying to let the weeds (tares) grow among the wheat since in the end he will harvest the wheat and burn the rest (yeah I know, *based*), but some formerly heretical churches have actually re-entered communion with Christianity (see: Trinitarianism) after originally being founded by a heretic due to engaging with Christian ideas.

Being very simplistic, theological conservatives in Christian circles are often stunted from not being able to think very well through any lens except their native cultural philosophy which they think forms a plain reading of the Bible. Cringe, yes. But meanwhile, theological liberals in Christian circles are often completely castrated and overwhelmed by the fact that they keep allowing in atheists, heretics, and unrepentant sinners. So there is a lot of danger in both being too quick to cut down what we see as a weed without thinking about if it is actually new wheat we do not recognize, and in not cutting down the likely weeds when they are crushing the obvious wheat to death. (God wants us to be good proximate stewards.)
on schism

The compromise is pretty much a network society system where people can amplify what they think is good, and no one can simply downrank to the point of utter oblivion something they think is bad. Much closer to the network society concept than anything else, of course, though Twitter sometimes acts like a pope giving shadow bans.

Funny how much culture seems to be a projection of attentional wells. Like idea-space has these little gravity pockets that clump things together.

Funny that things like synchronicities can direct or create these wells. It's like there's a bit of a hypochondriac in everyone that can be invoked just pointing at a well. The context maintainer part of living things can't help but look in these wells to survive.

With memetics, culture can probably be pretty easy to change since you can see all these wells. Though the danger is you don't know how it will change since feedback loops and circular dependencies are the norm.

First Order Chaos
An unpredictable phenomenon that doesn’t respond to predictions.

For eg. The weather is a chaotic system. It can’t be predicted with total accuracy because of the huge number of variables involved. However, the weather does not change based on the predictions about it.

Second Order Chaos
An unpredictable phenomenon that responds to predictions about itself.

For eg. stocks and politics—if someone predicts that a certain stock will rise 20% tomorrow, then people will react to the prediction. This can cause demand and hence the price to go up today, which can make the prediction incorrect.

Second order chaos is, in a sense, the opposite of a self fulfiling prophecy. By making a prediction, people will react to the prediction and make it not come true.

Copied from <https://gyanl.com/chaos>

Actually I recall some discussions here likening human behaviour to weather patterns that I disagree with because it's the wrong order of chaos.

Also I think superstition as a concept works better in theory than in practice because humans, like the market, function as a second order chaotic system.

Or at least how I would do it, and I'm not going to pretend like I understand your whole situation. It's a negotiation with the external for your continued growth and existence.

In general, I would agree LLM are not great at novelty, but I think this holds true for most humans too.

Suspect this lack of novel creation is a result of LLM being static/dead and it's life tends to be between major releases. However, you can see using agent mode that it can interact with the world and learn new things as long as it fits in the context window.

In short, AI is limited (by design) to not produce too many A's and has been optimized for B's

yeah this is the reason why I want to do it this way. I don't want to keep investing in different people and then each project only gets halfway and stops

what you want instead is to design such that each step can be scaffolding for the next piece. That way you can have a lot of "drive by" contributors who only do one thing, or work for a few weeks, and it doesn't block others work

like, everyone maintaining a blog that has an RSS feed is already a good thing that each person should do (your Obsidian can be that, but substack also works, or bluesky, every bsky account has an RSS feed)

now, someone else can build a visualization interface/social media that consumes your RSS feeds. Multiple people can be working on that

if someone does that, it inspires a lot of people to get together and collect their RSS feeds/put their content together. But if the guy making the viz disappears or wants to lock in the content or w/e, the community doesn't lose

the core idea I made explicit with that experiment inspired by your God conjecture is that: everything evolves through small simple steps, but the moment an observer starts watching, new patterns appear that can’t be reduced back to the simple rules.

You look at a cloud - it means something to you

You look long enough -  the act of observing changes what you see

Keep watching -\ the system reaches a tipping point

Suddenly it becomes something new
this experiment was like the universal loop behind this process.
it shows how simple rules grow into complex structures and how watching those structures adds another layer of evolution.
this may not be an A for must of us here we've all seen a cellular automata  in action, the key idea is making the *watching* part explicit to reveal the added layer of evolution from that watching.
The model merges three things.

(A) Irreducible Processes

Some things can’t be simplified.
If you want to know what they do, you must watch them unfold.

( Wolfram’s idea of computational irreducibility.)

(B) Observer-Dependent Evolution

The act of observing doesn’t just record - it changes the system.

my model states:

Perfect observation converts freedom into “mass”  an accumulated weight or density in the idea / mind / system.

i call this  “featherfall gravity.”


(C) Collapse Thresholds

As observation accumulates, the system reaches a critical density (“mass”), and:

the pattern collapses

it reorganizes

a new structure emerges

the claim is that the observer is a computational subsystem whose act of observation constrains the space of possible evolutions in the Ruliad.

Well because it's a bundle of signals:

1) on one hand it's signalling that you are capable of generating good/true things + you've reinforced those productive/good parts of your model
2) on the other hand the opportunity cost of simply learning the already produced thing faster instead of generating it which tends to be costlier is bad. 

But I would try to just accept the imperfect information we all swim in as finite beings and take each of these moments as opportunities to adjust your weights: ie: reinforce the positive parts, and fix/improve the aspects that will allow you to be more efficient moving forward

went offline for a bit there.

took last week off to record a bunch of music with friends from all over (Portland, Chicago, Nashville)! We learned and recorded 14 songs in 6 days. I can already feel a massive shift in my own approach to songwriting (even though I didn't write any of these songs). 

Basically, now that I've recorded an album with a bunch of other people who are into writing/recording, I understand that bringing a polished idea to the studio isn't strictly necessary. If you trust your collaborators, you can often bring 60% of a song, and get a better result. This is actually huge, since the first 60% of writing is both the quickest and the most enjoyable.

Working idea: Songwriting as a twofold process.

1. conceptualization: internal, indulgent, skeletal. Hone either a broad strokes emotion with underdetermined musical/lyrical content OR develop the latter without the former.

2. reflection: Observe reactions. Playful. Let your collaborators either affirm or frustrate your conceptualization. If you trust them, you can all determine the path the leads to a good tune. Doesn't even matter if your conceptualization sticks around. If it does, that means you said something meaningful. If not, you gain new understanding of the world / understanding of how the world understands you. Win win.

Anyways, it was great. Got to play a bunch of sax and piano, my loves. Tracking some vocals this week, mixing with room mate, good fun.

As for the software, taking a week or so away from it has been great for me. The second I looked at it again, I had two substantial insights about how to improve. Naturally, that means my code is partially broken once more, but looking at my code, I get the sense that I'm addressing the last of the big inefficiencies.
I'm in Waterloo, Ontario!
https://pages.markdown.space/sunriseoath/thresholds-divine-kairos

Also linked an article critical of the lecture by Michael Crichton 🙂

Apparently Crichton's take is widely cited by people who are against the consensus on climate science! I definitely heard some of the talking points from someone when I was still of a much more liberal pro-secular-institution bent.

Reading the comments arguing about the Crichton lecture is fascinating because there are clearly the different streams of thought that Defender once mentioned with regards to views on science according to political bent. One side is very focused on how the literature internally has checks and balances, and the other side is very focused on how the literature can be influenced or disrupted from the outside.

It is all at once true that internal incentives can achieve some kind of equilibrium that prices in the truth (as the truth itself is valuable and helps those who accord with it win!), and that forces from outside the system can knock the internal equilibrium into misalignment for potentially an entire generation.

People in that thread keep switching between two positions without admitting it. The *motte* is the safe claim: “climate models are just physics and math, so critiquing them is like critiquing gravity.” But the *bailey*—the part actually used in public—is treating model outputs as if they were empirical findings, not structured guesses shaped by assumptions and tuning. So when someone raises concerns about how models get rhetorically inflated or selectively amplified, defenders retreat to the motte and pretend the criticism was against basic science itself. The result is a built-in slippage where hypotheses get smuggled in as “results,” and any challenge to that overreach gets framed as anti-science rather than a critique of how institutions use models.

There is a culture thing in online Christianity where pretty much no one gives sources for anyone unless purely for polemical reasons (like "You are just repeating this one guy's arguments") because ultimately all good work is for the kingdom. I think that this is pretty much where I fall, too. In the end, any "insight" I write up can directly be traced to my personal story interfacing with Christian thought (and esp. the scriptures), so it is hard for me to want credit for anything when God is clearly not going out of his way to take the credit.

I have a much higher degree of respect for copyleft than for the creative commons for the simple reason that the former leans more into the death of the author than the latter. I like the idea that someone releases their work completely no strings attached almost as if it directly entered the public domain much more than I like the idea that you still have to cite the right person.

I like Twitter because I can just quote tweet someone interesting and if someone follows my self-quotes far enough, they can see anything that plausibly qualifies as a source. I do try to take note of what I read and what sparked certain insights so I can find my way back after, but I really dislike the idea that attribution is to be expected rather than being a situationally useful tool for improving information pathways.
John going on an ego trip over his prophet incentive writings from a few years ago is not a mark of a person with a healthy relationship to reality, in my eyes.

Let it be known that I would be extremely happy if someone took all my writings, slapped their name on it, and published then distributed it to great acclaim. This would further my mission and probably would actually result in me benefitting more than if someone could only promote my thoughts through direct attribution.

If it were somehow relevant that I wrote an excerpt, for example if there were some debate on how a section might be most productively read, it might be relevant that I am the proximate author. But God is always the ultimate author anyway, so even when we say that we wrote something, we are running the risk of taking credit on a much more dangerous scale than mere human to human plagiarism.
Specialists are a way of sidestepping the collaboration problem. Everyone stays in their lane, become tools instead of the amorphous blob swarm

The history of the Christological debates is kinda related to the semantic embedding of "Jesus of Nazareth" relative to humanity and divinity. Even though Christ being fully man and fully divine was established early on, whether one focuses on the humanity of Christ or the divinity of Christ does actually result in different formulations of doctrine (many of which were found to be heretical). A simplication that Redeemed Zoomer gives in this video is that reflecting on the twoness of Christ leads to considering the humanity of Jesus, and reflecting on the oneness of Christ leads to considering the divinity of Jesus. (One of the things I keep coming back to is a throwaway line from this video that Jesus is technically a divine person, not a human person, despite having both a divine and a human nature.)

15 step guide to find out what you actually want in life

Step 1: Gather things you already have
Step 2: Figure out what you can do with the things
Step 3: Do the actions that you can do with the things
Step 4: Figure out how doing those things make you feel
Step 5: Ration the thing that you like doing so you don’t run out too fast
Step 6: Build a habit out of doing things that feel good
Step 7: Find other people who do the same things as you
Step 8: Realise that more cool things exist out there and cool people do cool things
Step 9: Talk to other people who do the same things as you and see if you can get more fun things to do.
Step 10: Get other people to join in on your fun activity
Step 11: Start writing about your activities as an activity log
Step 12: Figure out how to combine the activities you enjoy doing
Step 13: Come up with a name for this new activity that you do
Step 14: Understand what you’re doing so that you can explain it to others better
Step 15: Inform and celebrate with the world about what you’re doing

I have a task for you guys I think <@257607342806269954> <@1024417977111691305>, I think Kanro is testing the ability of our intelligence network

## God as One and Three

1. If God is truly one, what would go wrong if the Father, Son, and Spirit were simply different roles God plays at different times?

2. How can the Son obey the Father during Jesus’ earthly life without being less divine than the Father?

3. Why does Christian teaching resist the idea that each person of God does separate work independently?

4. What would change about prayer or worship if God were only one person rather than a living relationship?

## Jesus as Fully God and Fully Human

5. Why would it be a problem if Jesus were a blend of God and humanity rather than fully both?

6. What would be lost if Jesus had a human body but not a fully human mind and will?

7. Why does it matter that Jesus could genuinely choose obedience, trust, and endurance?

8. In what sense can Mary be called the mother of God without implying that she existed before God?

## Goodness, Evil, and Reality

9. Why does Christian teaching resist the idea that evil is a “thing” equal in power to good?

10. What does it mean to say that something is good simply by existing at all?

11. How can the world still be called good even when it is clearly broken and damaged?

12. Why does Christian belief insist that God does not become more loving over time?

## Human Brokenness and Salvation

13. What is at stake if humans are assumed to be able to reach God on their own without help?

14. Why would salvation become unstable if God waited to see who would choose Him first?

15. How can a person be declared right with God while still needing deep moral transformation?

16. What would happen to assurance if acceptance by God depended partly on moral improvement?

## Integration and Big-Picture Coherence

17. Why must Jesus truly represent humanity *and* truly bring God’s life to humanity for salvation to work?

18. Why does Christian teaching insist that God is not made up of parts or dependent pieces?

19. How does the belief that goodness and existence are closely connected shape how Christians view creation?

20. Why does Christian theology stretch human language about God instead of discarding it altogether?
These questions are somewhat openended, so I wonder what you might make of them!

Ok, after a good bit of time testing everything against reality, I've come to a couple of realizations. 1. the Yes Pen physical device doesn't have demand yet because I haven't shown anyone the possibilities with it effectively yet and I don't have the skills to make it look great. As an addendum, I realized that trying to raise an ecosystem fund would make sense if I was established enough with trust, but I don't have that yet. So it's a waste of time. Instead, I can simply create a network structure that focuses the attention of money on a specific point.

So I've been working on MetaSPN Training Camp. It's a way for people to develop their own game that showcases their unique strengths, and helps them develop their comparative advantage in a hypercompetitive environment. Weeks 1-4 are developing and stress testing your own game model and weeks 5-12 are all about deploying it and measuring the effectiveness of it.

The key is giving people a way to leverage their strengths effectively, and they can't do that unless they can make them legible. It's what I've struggled so much with.

So I figured out how to help people become more legible by turning everything into sport.

On good outcomes for everyone: I assume this would mean good outcomes for everyone within an aligned project, because for those misaligned, what is good and true and beautiful is evil and false and ugly to their doomed eyes. So it is quite important to select for those who like what is good and true and beautiful. The more one tries to actively align with those who are misaligned (rather than trying to move toward that common point of true alignment), the more one is *hindered* by cooperation.

Pursuing the good and the true and the beautiful has an important element of schism to it. This is where I am a much more divisive thinker than I think many in ORI tend to be. From the start I have supported way more visible signs where we make it clear how we stake our positions on what is truly aligned vs. unaligned. This is necessary for preventing something false from ruling the scene without it even being named. (Invisible sin is necessarily unrepented sin.)

"align the aligners"

Concurrent frameworks allow for emergently agreed-upon compartments. Soon as people agree on a compartment, then it already exists. This also prevents reifying constructs that are not demonstrably useful, or at least lets alternatives compete against each other in some context and also point to where we might congregate next. 

This happens to be pretty much what theology is about: making it clear what a group can agree on is true and useful and necessary. 

Also, an important idea for alignment is to focus on the ideal and not the people. To meet at the kingdom, we must each depart from where we last saw each other. If we want to go to true north, there is no use in trying to move right next to the person living at magnetic north; we need to eventually *depart* from the person at magnetic north, and hope that they will be where we wanted to end up in the end.

A big risk for any organization is where aligning to the organization requires departing from what is actually *more* true. This is where keeping a looser hold on super downstream concepts and divisions is good, but keeping a tight hold on very fundamental concepts and visions is utterly necessary. One may let the branches and buds grow free, but one must also agree on what is the trunk.
Exactly

This also reminds me of how startups are often informally organized not around set roles, but around the mission statement itself. (Some companies have their CEO as a living mission statement, which only works while they are alive and sane.) The exact road map is much less important than the direction.

It is notable that the New Testament was written in direct reaction to contemporary concerns, and then was used to extrapolate beyond the immediate audience.

The Nicene Creed itself is very much a reaction to contemporary concerns. It sparked a whole Trinitarian and Christological debate lasting centuries, but was originally just speaking against the popular teachings of Arius.

So in some sense I am suggesting not an open canon (what is actually the revelation itself is not up for further addition even though we might in theory have it wrong), but rather an open exegetical literature.

In Discord literally any message can be linked to. I think that we should start writing up messages that summarize other messages and "bind" them together, and then essentially get a full editorial history of the source writings that make up later proper statements. We need a firm foundation on top of which everything else is built. The church is a project where instead of telling people exactly what to do, it tells everyone what to build their entire lives on. The alignment happens not in the canopy, but at the roots.

<@1262608101270622230> and I seem to have similar thinking on this. It is utterly intractable to have a top-down system where every single division and delegation must be controlled. (Network-based societies in the Calvinist model happen to rely on using everyone’s discernment together to generate an intelligent market.) Instead, the thing to control is the very confession and profession necessary for entry into the community. Unironically, what ORI needs more of are discrete creeds to which people assent. Like, for example, I would be interested in seeing how many people in this server would affirm the Nicene Creed and would be willing to have a role saying that they bind themselves to the language of this creed. This actually would, in my eyes, promote many useful network effects. I know I trust someone more if they affirm the same creeds as me.

I suggest that likeminded people in this server start to create minimally viable creeds that show their shared commitments, and then we see who signs on to which creeds so that we have a visible voting-with-your-feet count of where people are at with their commitments and their own conceptions of alignment. I will openly promote the open affirmation of the uncontroversial Apostles' Creed among Christians in this server.

I think that reifying the existing narratives is a necessary component of spiritual warfare, if that makes sense. There is definitely an element where a creed itself is a tool of proselytization (due to the power of the network effects), but there is also an act of public service inherent to legibilizing allegiances.

Narrative control can be dangerous when one begins to arrogate the ultimate say. In the end, Christians affirm that only God tells his story. We might have strong assurances regarding the interpretation of that story, but in the end the source of all authority *is* God who is outside of us.

I would say that mainline Protestantism has the same decentralization as Eastern Orthodoxy without having the idea of an infallible church (which I think is wrong). Eastern Orthodoxy is of course correct that reality is not interpretation of words but actual existence with which we are engaged (with salvation being ultimately reconciliation with existence). I do not support how evangelicals go about their faith in effectively a gnostic way.

This post by Danielle Fong about her convincing Peter Thiel of the importance of vertically-integrated monopolies is both on the mesa level an interesting thesis to consider (*How can ORI build a self-sustaining ecosystem that stakes out its own neighborhood? Will it just happen in the same way that a planet carves out its own “neighborhood” over the course of orbit?*), but also a great case study of how speaking to well-connected and powerful people can shape society. ([X](https://x.com/DanielleFong/status/2001010843610386836?s=20))

The mainline Protestant churches in America are in full communion with each other despite existing theological disagreements, which is quite similar to the Christian ecumenical model of Eastern Orthodoxy. The only real difference is on the question of church infallibility and on whether any specific institution (either centralized or simply emergent and visible) is the actual gatekeeper of salvation. It is actually possible to formulate mainline Protestantism in a way that sounds surprisingly Eastern Orthodox. Like, technically, I believe there is no salvation outside the church and that participation in the Christian life is (conditionally) necessary for salvation.

There are also plenty of mystics in Western Christianity (Catholicism and Protestantism), and I think that this is mostly hidden by the fact that many people due to the influence of other Eastern religions believe that using firm distinctives is the realm of the intellectual rather than the spiritual. This of course falls apart as a heuristic when we see just how many distinctives Eastern Orthodoxy itself uses on very foundational doctrines, and also when we consider how people do come to Christ through intellectual engagement that subsequently opens the heart (which in the Old Testament is actually not separate from the mind, the seat of rationality).
Defender everything is in the mirror

If we equate Protestants with Baptists (who form a plurality of those who might self-identify as Protestant despite mostly not actually being mainline or institutional and also are often way more concerned with purity of beliefs than with the Christian life), then I think your criticism holds. When I think of Protestants, I often *exclude* Baptists and non-denominationals (the latter being theologically mainly the former) on the basis that they are anarchic schismatics.

This will sound very motivated because it is, but I think that looking at what concerns the early church was occupied with might give an idea of what constitutes axioms versus downstream from axioms. Like, at some level it does come down to something almost religious; to be aligned to the same reality, a group needs to agree on what reality is and not at least outright hate using the same language about this reality.
https://sunriseoath.substack.com/p/christian-theology-as-foundation

There is of course some amount of overlap between what is foundational (in terms of it appearing in a proper canon) and something being axiomatic (in terms of it being an irreducible fact or theorem within a system). I think finding your place in the network would map to the idea of Christian practical theology of respecting authority. This is both something that is found within the scriptures themselves (Romans 13) but also in a more formal theology is downstream from God having authority and acting through his creation. One could reach the teaching of Romans 13 just from theology (and this is exactly the beauty of theology, that it can be used to analyze, expand, and generalize the teachings of Jesus and the apostles), but it also is already just in the text and is beyond dispute wtihin the Christian community.

There is a lot of interplay between these two ideas: (1) What is a text (instantiation of ideas and worldview) one could choose such that someone rejecting it as authoritative, almost to the point of infallibility, would be a strong signal of them being misaligned? (2) What is an idea (not contained within words but rather expressed through them) that if not used would result in a worldview incompatible with one’s project, incl. the interpretation of any other text or sign?

Insofar that a sign maps to its significance, the sign is the significance. Someone rejecting the words of the Apostles' Creed is, to me, a clear sign of someone rejecting the meaning of the Apostles' Creed (which as I interpret it contains some of the most central doctrines of the scriptures) and would be the basis for me not claiming community with someone, because the very way that I interpret words would not be compatible with theirs. If I otherwise thought that they understood English but they read the same foundational words I read and think that they are false, it would mean to me that their disagreements with me are even more foundational and paradigmatic than the differences between languages. Which is huge. (Meanwhile, someone reading a translation of the Apostles' Creed in Sanskrit and agreeing with it would lead me to great assurance of being on the same page even if we cannot directly communicate with words, if I have assurance that the translator both affirms what I believe and also clearly states that he considers the two translations to be equivalent across their two languages.)

So to me, organizational things like reifying the process of legibilizing one's own place within ORI is necessarily consequent to establishing the core mission toward which all processes is actually supposed to point. It is often easier to express our vision through describing the processes that carry out the vision as we see it, and in this sense the processes can even be a sort of language. But the important core is the actual mission. (If someone broke all the rules you established for the sake of your mission but completely satisfied your mission, of course this would be better than someone following your rules but without wanting to achieve your mission.)

Less important than grammar and syntax is the establishment of semantics. It happens to be that people already have shared languages, and so we should use those shared languages to as neatly categorize meaning as we can. At least for Christianity, a part of this is focusing directly on those doctrines that are considered difficult to casually affirm without thinking. (For example, the virgin birth and the resurrection of Christ.) In a secular context, this might be something like "Fundamentally, existence as it truly is is good." In the current day, this is considered a controversial view.

From ChatGPT:

**ELI5 summary: Constitution, Canon, Community**

Think of a group like a game club.

**1) The canon (the rulebook).**
This is the *text everyone agrees to treat as authoritative*. In Christianity it’s Scripture and creeds; in a project it’s a constitution, charter, or founding documents. The point isn’t that the words are magic, but that the group agrees: *“If we argue, we argue from here.”* Rejecting the rulebook is a loud signal that you’re playing a different game.

**2) The axioms (the meaning behind the words).**
These are the *deep assumptions* the text points to: what is real, what is good, who has authority, what the goal is. You can often derive these ideas by reasoning—but the canon pins them down so they’re not endlessly renegotiated. That’s why Romans 13 matters: authority isn’t just a clever inference; it’s explicitly named and therefore settled inside the community.

**3) The community (the players).**
A community is simply the people who *accept the same rulebook and aim at the same meaning*. Shared language matters less than shared understanding. Two people in different languages who affirm the same creed are aligned; two people reading the same words but denying their meaning are not. At that point the disagreement is deeper than vocabulary—it’s about reality itself.

**4) Constitution vs. process.**
Processes are how the game is played day-to-day. The constitution and canon are *why the game exists at all*. Someone who breaks rules to serve the mission may still be aligned; someone who follows every rule while rejecting the mission is not. Rules serve meaning, not the other way around.

**5) Why this feels “religious.”**
Any durable group must answer quasi-religious questions: *What is real? What is good? What are we for?* A canon is how a community makes those answers legible and stable. Without that, you don’t have disagreement inside a project—you have different projects using the same words.

In short:

* **Canon/constitution** fixes the reference point.
* **Axioms/semantics** are the reality the text points to.
* **Community** is whoever commits to both.

Lose any one of the three, and the group dissolves into noise.

Someone who accepts the canon and community without the axioms is a mere performer or reciter. They do a service via repeating the things people know, but might not have the deep understanding needed to aid true faith or to develop the mission beyond the clearest signs.

Someone who accepts the axioms and community without the canon is a babbler. They might be able to help with great things and spur great thought, but they often will undermine the very foundations of the faith through muddying what is and is not the source for the rule of faith. (Think of how many expressions people will assume are biblical or Christian but which are not.) Cultural Christians often fall here. 

Someone who accepts the canon and axioms without the community is a recluse or a hermit. They might seek to be a great tool for the mission and might even produce materials that others can use to serve the mission, but they also hamstring themselves and others through avoiding full participation. This ironically will undercut the very mission outlined in the canon and axioms. They might appeal to lack of epistemic certainty about whether others are part of the same mission, but then lean toward being arrogant in believing they have it figured out without the checks that a living community provides. Many evangelicals actually fall here, as do some Christians who struggle with church institutions.

* **Canon** guards *what counts*.
* **Axioms** guard *what it means*.
* **Community** guards *how it is lived and corrected*.

# Mimi's 1st Draft for Learning How to Diffuse and Refract Hostile Engagement.
("Don't stay home without it!!!")

## The Core Principle (Internet Aikido)

> **Do not oppose the anger.  
> Do not submit to it.  
> Do not ignore it.  
> Translate it into structure.**

Anger online is almost never about dominance.  
It’s about **unacknowledged specificity**.

Rude people feel:

- unheard
- dismissed
- abstracted over
- talked _around_

So Aikido doesn’t “win.”  
It **re-anchors the conversation to a concrete axis** where heat loses leverage.

---
## The Four Aikido Moves

### 1. **Name the Tension (Without Judging It)**

You acknowledge _what_ they’re reacting to, not _how_ they’re reacting.

This disarms escalation without validating hostility.

> “You’re asking a very concrete question.”

Not:

- “Calm down”
- “Why are you so angry”
- “You’re being rude”

Those are counter-force.

---
### 2. **Compress the Problem**

Anger feeds on sprawl.  
You reduce the issue to a **single, sharp formulation**.

> “The real issue is the gap between income interruption and debt obligations.”

This signals competence and seriousness.

---
### 3. **Shift From Outcome to Mechanism**

Most fights happen at the level of _beliefs_.  
Aikido moves to _process_.

> “There are only a few mechanisms that historically handle that gap.”

Now the debate is technical, not emotional.

---
### 4. **Offer Structure, Not Salvation**

You don’t promise solutions.  
You map the terrain.

This preserves agency on both sides.

---
---
## Why This Works

### ✔ Not Submissive

- Does not apologize
- Does not placate
- Does not accept blame

### ✔ Not Dominant

- No dunking
- No shaming
- No “gotcha”

### ✔ Not Indifferent

- Directly engages the core concern
- Names the real issue precisely

### ✔ Aikido-Accurate

- Receives force
- Redirects to structure
- Grounds the exchange

The anger has nowhere to land.

---
## Meta-Rule to Remember

> **Rudeness is usually a protest against abstraction.**

So the move is always:  
**less vibe, more mechanism**  
**less moral posture, more causal clarity**

After contemplating your work, I think that we are working on the exact same thing, toward the same end, following the same philosophy and process and sources, but you're building logic whereas Ive been building circuits.

You're constructing inside the chips, and I'm stringing chips together.

I've been playing around with making these "Integrated Chips" 
Or maybe call them "Interpreted Chips",
but I'm starting to realize that as more of an architect of systems, Ive been working up, down and across the stack.

The "stack" in question can be described no other way besides a mimetic computer.

It mimics and mirrors its environment, and it inteprets and interpolates its methods.

So, you're working at the base "Hardware" level.

I've been working across the hardware and software.

If this thing were to be truly built, we would need to extend the metaphor, and get talent whose logic and technical skills can be translated into the philosophy and ontology of this device.

But even now, we can do alot by constructing a simple device.
Think of it like a redstone machine in minecraft, except crafted through semiotic machinery.

A natively virtual operating system and platform.

One way that I explained it was that it's a mental prosthetic.

Well, more like an augmentation. I'm trying to find the complete set of all the possible chips so that people can work on unexplored regions.

Just by nature of being in this metafield we're belong nowhere and everywhere. Metallurgy in a world of tool makers.

For ANY situation, ask:
What's the STUFF involved? (Substance)
What's CHANGING? (Process)
Who's CONNECTED? (Relation)
What do we SEE? (Manifestation)
What's the SITUATION? (Context)
How are people SEEING it? (Perspective)
What's the PATTERN? (Pattern)
How STRONG are feelings? (Intensity)
What DON'T we know? (Mystery)
This is the first layer that's supposed to decompose it.
Like instead of asking 5W 1H or the SWOT analysis

There is a lot I'm excited to implement and I only have 50 or so years left,
so I need to organize my thoughts a bit. Here are the big ideas I can't wait to make real.

Data teleology - Some node based programming environments obscure what's happening
under the hood with a flat UI (Pure Data). We will make data life cycle explicit and functional.

Port.ConnectionType{HORIZONTAL, VERTICAL} - this semantic let's the user decide if what's being sent
can only be read (HORIZONTAL), or read and written to / sent to output (VERTICAL)

HORIZONTAL means that piece of data is at the end of its life cycle, so we can branch an arbitrary
number of times and not worry about aliasing. Imagine many straight dotted lines flowing out from a
single source. A bunch of Operators sharing a reference.

VERTICAL means the value being carried is being mutated and cannot branch without an explicit
CopyOperator (one input, arbitrary number of outputs that create copies of the input). Imagine it as
a solid, physical cable affected by gravity and motion. This represents the real, physical thing
that we are changing.

Port.OutputType{GENERATOR, MUTATOR} - this semantic tells us if the output of an Operator is the same
piece of data as one of the inputs (x += y implies we are changing x by adding y to it, so input 1 and
output 1 of AddOperator share an identity. But a ListOperator, for instance, might take 2 arguments,
one for size and another for type. This creates a new data identity at the output that is dependent upon,
but fundamentally separate from, its input).
That sounds like a great move and both  get to hang out while doing core work as you described. Standby I want to share a paper to give context to the above.

Both connection type and output type are enforced by contracts in each operator, that get communicated to its ports. For instance, if an output doesn't have a connection, the user can decide if connections coming out of it are HORIZONTAL or VERTICAL. But on the input side, again with AddOperator, you need a VERTICAL input. Some object has to persist from the input to the output. Unless, of course, the Operator is a
GENERATOR. ListOperator, for instance, could take two HORIZONTAL inputs and work fine.

These two combine in what I hope will be a really beautiful way. If I can execute properly, we gain lots
of power, flexibility, and a wonderfully romantic visual scheme, at little cost in terms of complexity /
learning curve.

As far as learning curve goes, these two features don't complicate much for beginners; the user can
always use vertical connections if they want to and nothing breaks. The only real restriction introduced
is that each output can only have one vertical connection, which, actually, will gently push the user into
understanding reference vs value. They aren't going to want to keep adding a CopyOperator every time they want
to send some useful variable to many places!

What I'm really excited about is the wonderful representation we acheive through this. We now
have access to a bunch of subgraphs that tell us which Operators are the boundaries between meaningful chunks of data.
That means we have the ability to color our graph in a way that expresses the story our data tells.

Here's my biggest trick. Imagine a sprawling, directed acyclic graph that is responsible for producing a single image.
Now, imagine you remove every HORIZONTAL connection. What are you left with? What is the shape of the main thread? Did it split
into 2 big threads? Are there islands now? What does it mean that there are islands disconnected from the main thread? What
does the size of the island mean?

It becomes immediately clear that the islands separated from the main thread are inputs to that main thread. They posess
no data that persists through that thread, but they influence it. This a powerful thing to have built into the environment.
What we are left with are all of the tiers of abstraction that exist in digital signal processing, from low
level machine humming that flips a bit and what the heck does that mean for my image, who the hell knows? to ok, that chunk
applies a blurring filter to my image. That chunk there combines with the blurred image to fix my relationship with my son etc..

I have lots to say about how I will implement iteration/loops as well, but this is enough for now.

Overall, this is a pretty significant departure from Pure Data, the main inspiration for this project. No feeback loops (DAG only.
We acheive feedback through read/write and a global data registry), strongly typed, overall the user is more restricted. My hope
is that it will be easier to reason about / more deterministic, and the visual will add depth to the workflow instead of complexity.

There's nothing inherently wrong with a probabilistic search and I think that there's a time and place to use it. Deterministic procedures should always take precedence over wild guesses.

The guessing introduces an asymmetry that doesn't give a logically deducible path back to the priors which creates a tension in the reasoning model. Heuristics help to guide the guesses but once again, part of the gradient descent school of thought.

<@1221182134950301706>  <@1024417977111691305> <@1085107383287693342> I've been processing our earlier syncs on mapping the information space and how ideas spread. I realised that most current tools focus almost entirely on velocity (how fast/far a meme travels).

But in systems architecture, velocity without mass is just noise. A lie travels faster than the truth because it has high entropy (it's cheap/disordered).

I’m aiming to add a Z-axis to that vision structural resonance.

While I finalize the core physics of the axiom engine, I’m seeing that we can use it to measure the synergy density of a thread or idea, measuring how "heavy" or "coherent" the signal is, not just how loud it is.

The vision: Instead of just visualizing nodes based on connection count (virality), we visualize them based on gravity.

High velocity + low density: Viral noise (attractive, but thermodynamically unstable).

Low velocity + high density: deep truth (slow burn, but structurally permanent).

High velocity + high density: Civilizational shifts (the event).

Once my prototype is stable (hitting 0.0000 entropy loss on logical rollback), I want to feed some of the ORI dataset into it. We could theoretically build a map that filters out the entropy and lights up the "structural holes" the places where two heavy concepts should connect but haven't yet.

We exist in a dynamic system. Time flows forward and everything is in motion.

For a particle to be stable - to persist as an identifiable thing - it must move forward with this flow. If it doesn't advance through time, it doesn't exist in our universe. This is the directional requirement: coherence requires flowing forward.

But here's the problem: if a particle's path through spacetime never repeats any pattern, it's indistinguishable from random noise. There's no consistent structure to identify. For something defined entirely by its movement through spacetime, *cycling - repeating a pattern - becomes its identity.*

So stable existence requires both: advancing through time AND tracing a repeating pattern.
When you rotate by 2π, you reach the same point again, but forward in time.  

Even when you draw a circle on paper, you're using three spatial dimensions and one time dimension. The representation might look 2D, but the actual act of drawing traces through 4D spacetime.
In 3D space, 3 points define exact position. Three legs make the most stable table. 3 is the number of discrete stability.

But we don't live in frozen 3D space, we live in flowing spacetime. **π encodes how to maintain that 3-point stability while the universe continuously moves forward.**


The divergence of π from 3 (approximately 0.14159...) encodes the pitch of the spiral we're following - how much we advance forward per cycle while maintaining identity.

This is why particles have helical structure in spacetime, the cycling (π) maintains identity. The forward flow (e) advances through time. Together they create persistent existence.

message:
**Mass measures how geometrically complex that helical pattern is** - how "knotted" the spiral structure.
The electron traces the simplest stable spiral - like the most basic loop. Easy to maintain, but also minimal resistance to change.

The proton's spiral involves five nested rotations (π⁵) with specific topology (coefficient 6) - like a complex knot with five intertwined loops. Much harder to disrupt without destroying the pattern entirely.

When you try to accelerate a particle, you're trying to change its helical path - make it trace a different spiral. The more complex the knot, the more resistant it is to this disruption, the resistance IS inertia and IS mass.

So the proton isn't 1836 times "heavier" in some substance sense, more like Its helical structure is 1836 times more complex, or harder to untie, harder to disrupt, harder to force into a different path.

Existence or fullness
The container makes it's first NOT "-"
Inside the container it creates a singularity, the NOT-existence itself inside existence, inside nothingness (-(1-(1))
NOT and NOT creates a relationship "="

2:

Existence and Singularity form a relationship "="
Both are NOT-zero
The + operation becomes coherent
(0, 1, -1) form a type

3: 
Inside NON-EXISTENCE → NOT propagates (making distinctions, creating multiplicity)
Inside EXISTENCE → = propagates (making equivalences, creating unity)
These are the SAME operation, nested,  the same propagation, viewed from both structures

NOT cycling inside inexistence (differentiation)
RELATIONSHIP cycling inside existence (integration)

Everything that exists in this system bootstraps out of the first triad (-1, 0, 1)
3 is perfect stability, 3 is minimum for chaos and adaptation

0 (void) → **NOT or -** (distinction) → 1 (existence)
↓
1 makes its first NOT → singularity inside
↓
**NOT + NOT → =** (relationship forms)
↓
1 = 1 (existence relates to itself)
↓
**1 + 1 → 2 (relationship has properties)**
↓
All properties now active → 1 = 3
