I oppose this one common attitude that distinctives are in the realm of the rational and not the mystical. We often assume mysticism with relaxed distinctives or outright confusion, but that is only because the type of mysticism that is easy to *parody* is the type that says vague aphorisms.

The system is directional (arrow of time, causality moves forward).

If each particle/observer traces a unique path forward (**required for causality**), we have a problem:
>Every path is unique
>No two moments repeat
>Like a transcendental number (e = 2.71828...) that never repeats its decimal pattern
>All paths look like random noise
>No relationships can be traced between them
>No identity can be established

To establish identity, the path must *cycle within its directional flow.*

LIke
→ (loop) → (loop) → (loop) →
Moment is unique (different), pattern repeats (=)

So CAUSALITY requires directionality
But IDENTITY requires cycling within that directional flow

It's kinda paradoxical, like a pure persistent signal (11111111111) carries no meaning, no change, nothing to observe

And geometrically that is exactly what we are looking out, some kind of helical structure

> I think my current prediction of your answer is something like:
> 
> - it's not that you can "change the past", is that the "event has not finished happening yet"
> 
> - which is why the metaphor you used is precise - writing a memoir of the war is tricky because you don't know what this particular battle meant, until the war is over
> 
> - to go further, we can say that the meaning of past events change, not because the past changed, but because their meaning IS in what they do in the future, what trajectories they lead to, what can be built on top of them
> 
> - you don't know that this one battle may have been "the most pivotal" or "completely irrelevant" until the war is over
> 
> (and I think this is related to something you said elsewhere that I can't find nowhere, about how the structure of a "story" is something kind of fundamental or points to something fundamental in reality? If Andres has you as a low score, it's not "objective". You may be in a high score by other nodes. The investor doesn't look at an anonymous aggregate score, you pick which chain(s) carry weight for you given the particular question

And what Defender would do in this system is curate these. In general truth converges. When these chains conflict, what I do is either shuffle information across between them to align them, or if they still don't converge, I can see who's stuck on what and why, and we can all make predictions on this
Another thing I do is find people who have a fast trajectory up these hierarchies, and invest in them

Last night I realized a pretty massive conceptual blunder that I'll try to frame as an epiphany, if only to build the motivation necessary to right my wrongs. <@1221182134950301706>  might be the only one with enough context to fully appreciate the fact that my cornerstone recursive definition is a bit f*cked. Here's why:

Modules are the things you see on screen and can interact with. Operators constitute nodes in the underlying graph that Modules expose. As in, adding modules to the screen and building connections is a visual stand in for changes to our graph. 

What does not change our graph, critically, is the nesting of Modules within eachother. We can drag and drop modules inside eachother to save space and create a useful abstraction, but all that composite module is doing is exposing specific ports of a global graph.

The key insight is that the graph is global. The way we nest Modules is just an arbitrary view of a singular graph.

This is all fine and dandy. The only problem is that I've defined Operator recursively and Module singularly (or whatever the conceptual absence of recursion is)! The correct approach is to define our data object (Operator) singularly and our view object (Module) recursively. 

This is the moment I learned to code. I have been very disciplined this go around in keeping  the data side decoupled from the UI side. Without that decoupling, I don't even know if this realization would have been possible for me to see, let alone implement the fix. 

Without getting too far in the weeds, the recursive definition of Operator has led to unnecessary pain. My implementation basically puts walls up around subgraphs. I've been spending lots of time building scaffolding to traverse these walls, and for no reason. The walls serve no purpose.

There is one graph, and the user can make nested views of that graph. The views are nested, not the graph. I'm happy I started using git so I can see how much of my code is gone after this. I'll guess 15%

One of the things I'm finding fascinating about what we're referring to as "female" cognition is that it's the type of perception that scans wide to be aware of threats.

My almost 2yo son seems to have a so much harder of a time seeing things that should be scary, particularly when some little mechanical gizmo has his attention. Seems so much more inclined to left brain capture.

https://x.com/i/status/2005707883267383524
So, I think I big chunk of the left hemisphere lock in humans is due to other humans purposely triggering it to prey on other humans.

I think this makes sense if you consider that observers are integrating more and different information from the past and future than other observers have access to.  If you're looking at a building, a historian, architect, or resident have very different reactions than a developer, construction worker, or mayor.  Because they're bringing different experiences and expectations to their viewing of the "same" building, they'll all have different impressions of what they're seeing.

the fix is going... surprisingly well. I spent 30 minutes reviewing the code in a few key areas and made a document outlining the changes I believed would be necessary. Made all those changes in about an hour. Then spent another half hour snuffing out little niblets.

So about 2 hours in, I have a program that compiles and is minimally functional (evaluates graphs that don't include any nesting). I'm guess the next part, establishing the recursive character of the display, will take a while longer. But honestly, I don't think it will be that bad. Here's what I'm thinking.

Global Map<Module, Window> windows. You key a Window with the Module that represents the boundary of that window. Then Window contains List<Module> modules. So, it's not explicitly recursive, but I don't think it needs to be.

One thing I'm pretty much certain of is that writing code for this will be waaayyy easier moving forward. This seems like a good topic for a first go at recording content. Instead of waiting til the project is shiny and stable and useful, drop in on a construction zone with a fat "this mistake cost years (coding fail compilation)".

an ideal scoring system I can imagine would not only *make it possible* to change one's opinion/rating, but actually make it the core value proposition of the system. if you gave someone an A, and then you realize you weren't aware of some factors and change your rating to C, then knowing *what* factors were they helps other people who follow your trails

yeah this is why the rating is given on a piece of information. At ORI we ask "have you gotten any recent A's?" 

or "I follow this guy because I get a lot of A's from him" or "that was a B for me" 

(and these mean specific things. An A is a world model update. B is a world model confirmation)

whoever is giving out a lot of B's is claiming their world model is accurate and confirmed by everything they see. If someone gives out B's and never gives out A's, he's saying he "knows everything/already predicted everything". 

Such a person I want to rise in the rankings, which would give him scrutiny (if he cannot give anyone A's, then he's probably crackpot, not brilliant)

The hell of a substrate can be seen both as the set of arrangements (and thus processes) that are selected out and so go extinct, and as the state of agents who identify with extinction. There is some straddle here between the selection process and then the locus of identity.

The true Christian hell really does rely on some sort of ability to identify; judgment is about wheat and chaff being separated, but hell is moreover about the judgment of *souls*, which have bodies and minds (without clear division though also without mixing).
If you consider the substrate to be the total set of positions instead of a specific simulation then you might consider hell to be the set of all useless simulations that will not be used again.

The super-intelligence discussions are kinda funny. The only way I can currently think about being scared of a super-intelligence would be one forming from a larger space before coming to our local space. 


Similar to how uncontacted tribes run into issues realizing they have lower global fitness and not much of an epistemic ladder to join the other side.

When I was in kindergarten, the teachers thought I was retarded and had me do an IQ test. They realized that I thought they were retarded and that's why I wouldn't listen to them.

This lack of being exposed to others with equal intelligence made me not trust the decisions of others even though they were valid because I often saw their reasoning was wrong and would have to redo the work myself.

So, as long as intelligence has contact with more limited intelligence, things will likely be gated. I would expect intelligent people to recognize this, but people who regularly encounter higher intelligences without an epistemic ladder are likely to be anxious and uncertain.
